request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"parse::devtools","name":"parse","one_liner":"Parse was a Mobile Backend-as-a-Service (MBaaS) platform for mobile developers that provided cloud data storage, identity management, push notifications, and custom cloud code, founded in 2011, acquired by Facebook in 2013, and shut down in January 2017.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both are developer-focused B2B SaaS products sold to developers on a usage-based or hosted-service basis. Parse used usage-metered monetization; the new pitch targets a hosted control plane with CLI tooling, a close structural match. The main difference is that the new pitch is explicitly multi-cloud and scoped to ephemeral environments rather than a general-purpose backend."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both serve the developer tools market and target developers (customer_type: developer) globally. However, Parse focused on mobile app developers needing a backend in 2011-2017, while the new pitch targets DevOps/platform-engineering teams needing preview/staging environment orchestration — adjacent but distinct sub-markets with different buyers and workflows."},"gtm":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both use a developer-led, bottoms-up GTM: a CLI or SDK for individual developers to adopt quickly, with expansion into broader org usage. Parse grew through developer community and word-of-mouth before acquisition. The new pitch's CLI-first approach mirrors this pattern closely."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Parse was post-YC, had $5.5M Series A funding, 20,000 developers within a year, and 500K apps by 2014 before its acquisition — it had achieved meaningful scale. The new pitch appears to be an early-stage concept with no stated traction. Stage similarity is limited."}},"why_similar":"Both are developer-focused hosted platforms (B2B SaaS) with usage-metered or hosted-service monetization, targeting developers globally. Both use a CLI/SDK-first bottoms-up adoption strategy where individual developers self-onboard before org-level expansion. Both abstract away complex infrastructure concerns (backend services for Parse; cloud provisioning for the new pitch), and both operate in the broader developer tooling ecosystem. The structural pattern — a managed control plane plus developer-friendly local tooling — is directly analogous.","where_diverged":"1. Sub-sector: Parse was a mobile backend platform (MBaaS) serving app developers storing data and sending push notifications; the new pitch targets DevOps/platform-engineering use cases around ephemeral preview environments, a distinct and more recent workflow. 2. Multi-cloud scope: The new pitch is explicitly cross-cloud-provider by design, whereas Parse was a single hosted service with no multi-cloud positioning. 3. Shutdown cause: Parse died because its acquirer (Facebook/Meta) strategically deprioritized it as a standalone commercial product — a corporate kill rather than a market failure. The new pitch has no acquirer risk at founding stage. 4. Era and market: Parse operated in 2011–2017 when MBaaS was nascent; the ephemeral-environment market in 2024+ benefits from Kubernetes, Terraform, and GitOps tooling as commoditized foundations.","failure_causes":["acquirer strategic deprioritization (Facebook shut down as non-core to social mission)","no sustainable standalone business post-acquisition","forced user migration with tight deadline destroyed trust","inability to compete once large cloud providers (AWS Amplify, Firebase) entered the MBaaS space","loss of independent product roadmap after acquisition","open-sourcing came too late to retain hosted-service user base"],"lessons_for_input":["Avoid acquisition as an exit if your product is the core infrastructure dependency for thousands of developers — a strategic shutdown by an acquirer destroys the ecosystem you built.","Build and own your enterprise/monetization layer before large cloud incumbents (AWS, GCP, Azure) replicate your feature set natively.","Invest in migration tooling and open-source optionality from day one so users are never locked into your hosted service with no escape hatch — this preserves trust if you ever need to wind down.","For CLI-plus-control-plane products, establish clear multi-tenant enterprise contracts early so revenue is diversified beyond small developer teams that churn quickly.","Validate that your ephemeral-environment abstraction layer remains valuable as underlying cloud providers add native preview/PR-environment features — Parse lost differentiation when Firebase and AWS Amplify commoditized its core value."]}
